text:
NUCLEAR MEDICINE BONE SCINTIGRAPHY REPORT

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margaret Okonkwo is a 63-year-old female with newly diagnosed right renal mass measuring 11.4 cm on recent CT imaging, with associated retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and bilateral pulmonary nodules highly suspicious for metastatic disease. Biopsy-confirmed clear cell renal cell carcinoma. Bone scan requested for osseous staging evaluation prior to definitive surgical planning.

TECHNIQUE:
Whole-body planar bone scintigraphy was performed following intravenous administration of 23.0 mCi (851 MBq) of technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate (Tc-99m MDP). Anterior and posterior whole-body images were acquired at approximately 3 hours post-injection using a dual-head gamma camera with low-energy, high-resolution collimation. Spot views of the skull, cervical spine, thoracic spine, lumbar spine, pelvis, bilateral hips, and bilateral knees were obtained as clinically indicated. Single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT/CT) of the lumbar spine and pelvis was performed for supplemental characterization of equivocal planar findings.

FINDINGS:

Skull and Calvarium:
No focal areas of abnormally increased or decreased tracer uptake are identified throughout the calvarium, skull base, or facial bones. Symmetric, homogeneous activity consistent with normal bony metabolism.

Cervical Spine:
Homogeneous radiotracer distribution throughout the cervical vertebral bodies and posterior elements. No discrete foci of abnormal uptake. Mild facet activity at C5-C6 and C6-C7 without discrete hot spots, felt most consistent with degenerative facet arthropathy in the context of age-appropriate changes. No scintigraphic features to suggest metastatic involvement.

Thoracic Spine:
Symmetric, evenly distributed tracer activity along the thoracic spine. No focal photopenic defects or areas of disproportionately increased uptake to suggest blastic or lytic osseous involvement. The posterior elements and costovertebral junctions demonstrate normal physiologic distribution.

Ribs:
No rib foci are identified that would raise concern for metastatic involvement. Several foci of mildly increased activity noted along the left 6th and 7th ribs laterally in a linear, contiguous pattern, highly characteristic of prior rib fractures at various stages of healing or chronic costochondral changes — this pattern is not typical of metastatic disease. The remainder of the rib cage demonstrates symmetric tracer uptake bilaterally.

Lumbar Spine:
Focal areas of mildly increased activity at L3-L4 and L4-L5 endplates and facet joints bilaterally. SPECT/CT correlation demonstrates these areas correspond to moderate endplate sclerosis and osteophyte formation on the CT component, entirely consistent with degenerative spondylosis. No evidence of cortical destruction, soft tissue mass, or paravertebral involvement. Findings are attributed to degenerative disc and facet disease rather than metastatic disease.

Sacrum and Pelvis:
No discrete foci of abnormal tracer uptake are identified within the sacrum, iliac wings, acetabula, pubic symphysis, or ischial tuberosities. Physiologic symmetric uptake at the sacroiliac joints bilaterally. No scintigraphic features suggestive of osseous metastases in this region. SPECT/CT of the pelvis demonstrates no destructive or sclerotic lesions.

Bilateral Hips and Proximal Femora:
Moderately increased, symmetric periarticular tracer activity at both femoral heads and acetabula bilaterally. CT correlation within the SPECT acquisition demonstrates narrowing of the hip joint spaces bilaterally with subchondral sclerosis and marginal osteophytes, consistent with bilateral hip osteoarthritis. No periosteal reaction or focal destructive lesion identified. These findings are degenerative in etiology.

Distal Femora, Knees, Tibiae, Fibulae, and Feet:
Symmetric physiologic activity throughout the bilateral lower extremity long bones. Normal periarticular activity at the knee joints. No suspicious focal lesions identified in the visualized distal appendicular skeleton.

Humeri, Elbows, Forearms, Wrists, and Hands (Visualized on Whole-Body Images):
No focal areas of abnormal uptake in the visualized upper extremity bones. Activity pattern is symmetric and physiologic.

Chest Wall and Sternum:
Sternum demonstrates uniform tracer distribution. No focal sternal lesion. Activity at the sternoclavicular joints is symmetric and non-focal.

Soft Tissue and Background:
Expected physiologic radiotracer distribution in the kidneys and bladder at the time of imaging. Notably, increased tracer activity is seen in the right renal bed region, consistent with the known large right renal mass. No pathologic soft tissue uptake outside of expected renal activity. Visualization of the left kidney with normal tracer excretion.

IMPRESSION:

1. No scintigraphic evidence of osseous metastatic disease throughout the axial or appendicular skeleton. The entirety of the bony skeleton surveyed demonstrates no focal areas of abnormal tracer uptake suspicious for metastatic involvement.

2. Degenerative changes at the lumbar spine (L3-L5 level) and bilateral hip joints, as confirmed on SPECT/CT correlation — these findings are entirely consistent with osteoarthritis and spondylosis and are not felt to represent metastatic disease.

3. Linear rib activity at the left 6th and 7th ribs laterally, most consistent with prior trauma or chronic degenerative costochondral changes. No aggressive features on the CT component. Correlation with clinical history is suggested; if the patient has no known prior rib trauma, short-interval follow-up plain radiograph of the ribs may be obtained at clinical discretion, though metastatic etiology is considered unlikely given the pattern and CT appearance.

4. Prominent activity in the right renal bed corresponding to the known large right renal mass.

In summary, this bone scan does not demonstrate evidence of skeletal metastatic involvement at the time of staging evaluation.

Signed electronically by:
Attending Nuclear Medicine Physician
